m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<stdio.h>
float x = 1.2;
void main ()
{
  printf ("%f ", x);
  printf ("%d ", y);
  my_func ();
}
int y = 5;
int my_func ()
{
  printf ("%f ", x);
  printf ("%d ", y);
  return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
You have to declare function and variable Y before main.
error: ‘y’ undeclared (first use in this function)

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is false about function main?

Options.OptionText: variables defined inside main become global variables.

m_Item1.Explanation: variables defined inside main have local scope inside main.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output of following code?
int f (int a)
{
  a >= 30 ? return (100) : return (200);
}

void main ()
{
  int b = 15;
  b = f (b + 15);
  printf ("%d", b);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: return is a statement. Return Statements cannot be used inside ternary operator in this way.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Trace the output of following program.
float display (int c)
{
  return (c++);
}

void main ()
{
  int k = 45;
  float f;
  f = display (k);
  printf ("%f", f);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Display function first return C value i.e 45 and then increase the value.The return type of display function is float and  we are using %f to print value it will display 45.000000.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Trace the output of following program.
#include <stdio.h>
int sum (int a, int b)
{
  if (a == b)
       return (a + b);
  else
      return (a - b);
}
void main ()
{
  int x = 0;
  x = sum (x, 1);
  printf (" %d", x);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: We are passing 0 and 1 to a sum function . In sum function a nd b are not equal hence it is returning a-b i.e. 0-1 . so answer is -1.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Trace the output of following program.
int cases(int a, char b)
{
    if (a == 'A')
         return a;
    else
        return b;
}
void main()
{
    char ch=' ';
    int i = 65;
    i = cases(i, ch);
    ch = cases(i, ch);
    printf(" %d %c", i, ch);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Ascii value of A is 65. When we use %d it prints 65 and %c prints character value.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of following program
#include<stdio.h>
int fun ();
int main ()
{
  int i;
  
  i = fun ();
  printf ("%d ", i);
  return 0;
}
int fun ()
{
  int i = 5;
  i++;
  return i;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Here i is int variable hence when we call function fun() it will return 6 which is stored in i (main function) and again second time when we call fun() function it will override previouse value hence output is 6.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
How many times “*” will be printed by the following recursive function where the value of
n is 3
void print_star( int n)
{
  int i = 0;
  if (n > 1)
    print_star (n - 1);

  for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
    printf ("*");

  printf (" ");
}

void main()
{
    print_star(3);
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
This is recursive call
Initial call  print_star(3) till n>1 it will call same function recutsively i.e. next call with print_star(2)  and then print_star(1)

in next statement the for loop will print "*" n times hence
print_star(1)  print *
print_star(2)  print * *
print_star(3)  print * * *
hence total 6 times it will print *.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
int f(int a)  
{ 
return a> 20 ? 10 : 20;  
}

void main( )
{ 
int b=5;
b=f(b+++15);
printf("%d", b);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: In the function call f(b+++15), the function parameter is b+++15 which is actually b++   +  15. Here b is first used in the expression as it is post incremented  hence the value passed to the function is (5+15=20). Now in function f, 20>20 is false and hence it returns 20 which is assigned to b again. Hence output is 20.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Trace the output of following program.
int add (int a, int b);
void main ()
{
  int x;
  x = add (21, 81);
  x = add (x, 1);
  printf ("%d", x);
}
int add (int a, int b)
{
  int q;
  q = a + b;
  return (a, b);
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
When we  have ',' seperated  value in return statement only right value get returen. Hence
add fuction returns 81 in first call and  in second call it will return 1. hence output will be 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Point out the error, if any in the while loop.
#include <stdio.h>
int main()
{
    void fun();
    int i = 1;
    while (i <= 5)
    {
        printf("%d ", i);
        if (i > 2)
            goto here;
    }

    return 0;
}
void fun()
{
    here: printf("It works");
}

Options.OptionText: Error: goto cannot takeover control to other function

m_Item1.Explanation: A goto statement provides an unconditional jump from the 'goto' to a labeled statement in the same function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program?
#include<stdio.h>
int i=0;
int fun ();
int main ()
{
  while (i)
    {
      fun ();
      main ();
    }
  printf ("Hello ");
  return 0;
}
int fun ()
{
  printf ("Hi");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Here the default value of i is 0 hence the While loop does not get executed and only printf statement get executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the program?
   #include <stdio.h>

   float cal (float value)
   {
     return (3 * value);
   }

   void main()
   {
     int a = 10;
     float b = cal ("123");
   }
m_Item1.Explanation:
Compiler does not found the cal method which will accept character/string value.hence result in error.
error: incompatible type for argument 1 of ‘cal’
note: expected ‘float’ but argument is of type ‘char *’

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Code:
#include <stdio.h>

  int i;
void increment (int i)
{
  i++;
}

int main ()
{
  for (i = 0; i < 10; increment (i))
    {
    }
  printf ("i=%d ", i);
  return 0;
}
What will happen when the program above is compiled and executed?
m_Item1.Explanation: In this example we have two copies of i once is global and another is in Increnment function. As local variable has higher priority than global ,increment function will increase value of local variable which is not reflected in global variable.Hence every time i get initialised to 0 after function call hence it will loop indefinitely.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output of following code?
int transfer (int a, int b)
{
  char t = 1;
  t = a;
  a = b;
  b = t;
  return a;
  return b;
}

void main ()
{
  int x = 2, y = 3;
  x = transfer (x, y);
  printf ("%d %d", x, y);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: transfer function returns value of a, second return statement will never get executed hence value of a is 3 which we catch in x variable and value of y is 3 hence output is 3,3.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a valid return type for a C function?
m_Item1.Explanation: String is not a built-in data type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the correct way to declare a function that takes no arguments and returns an integer in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C, a function that takes no arguments and returns an integer is declared as 'int function();'. The empty parentheses indicate no parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the return type of the function declared as 'float multiply(int a, double b);'?
m_Item1.Explanation: The return type is specified at the beginning of the function declaration. Here, it is 'float'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How are multiple parameters declared in a function in C?

Options.OptionText: By listing each on a new line

m_Item1.Explanation: In C, function parameters are listed inside the parentheses and separated by commas.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider the declaration 'char *getString();'. What does the asterisk (*) signify?

Options.OptionText: The function takes a pointer as an argument
The function returns a pointer to a character

m_Item1.Explanation: The asterisk indicates that the function returns a pointer to a character.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an incorrect function declaration in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'function int test();' is incorrect because the return type should precede the function name, without using 'function'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the function declaration 'void printMessage(char *msg);', what is 'msg'?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'msg' is a parameter name indicating that the function takes a pointer to a character (string) as an argument.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How would you declare a function 'computeAverage' that takes in two integers and returns a double?
m_Item1.Explanation: The correct declaration specifies the return type 'double' and lists the parameter types inside the parentheses.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the default return type of a function in C if not explicitly specified?
m_Item1.Explanation: In older versions of C, the default return type was 'int', but explicit declaration is recommended.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a function is declared as 'void updateValue(int *p);', what can be inferred about its parameters?

Options.OptionText: It takes a pointer to an integer

m_Item1.Explanation: The parameter 'int *p' indicates that the function takes a pointer to an integer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly matches a function's return type with its declaration: 'boolean checkStatus();'?

Options.OptionText: The function returns an integer value
The function returns a boolean value
The function returns a void type

m_Item1.Explanation: In the context of the question, 'boolean' is the return type as per declaration, although C typically doesn't have a boolean type by default, relying instead on integer-based logic.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider the function declaration 'long calculateSum(long a, long b);'. What data type does this function return?
m_Item1.Explanation: The return type 'long' is specified at the beginning of the function declaration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C, what keyword is used to declare that a function does not return a value?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'void' keyword is used in C to specify that a function does not return a value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What can be concluded about the function 'void swap(int x, int y);' regarding its return type?

Options.OptionText: It does not return any value

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'void' keyword indicates that the function 'swap' does not return any value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is a function 'divideByTwo' that takes a float and returns nothing correctly declared in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: Since the function returns nothing, 'void' is used as the return type, and 'float' is the parameter type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the following function prototype imply: 'double max(double num1, double num2);'?

Options.OptionText: The function takes two double arguments and returns a double

m_Item1.Explanation: The function takes two double precision floating-point values and returns a double precision floating-point result.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used in the declaration 'extern int function();'?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'extern' keyword is used to declare a function or a variable to have external linkage.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the following declaration signify in C: 'const int getValue();'?

Options.OptionText: The function returns a constant integer
The function takes a constant integer as parameter
The function does not return any value
The function returns a pointer to an integer

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'const' keyword indicates that the function 'getValue' returns a constant integer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the function declaration 'int add(int, int);', how many parameters does the function take?
m_Item1.Explanation: The function 'add' takes two integer parameters as indicated by the two inputs inside the parentheses.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given the function declaration 'char processInput(char c);', what is its return type?
m_Item1.Explanation: The return type is 'char', as specified at the beginning of the declaration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of a function declaration in C?

Options.OptionText: To define the functionality of the function
To specify the name and parameters to the compiler
To control the flow of a program

m_Item1.Explanation: A function declaration informs the compiler about a function's name, return type, and parameters, acting as a promise of its existence and usage in a program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following function declarations is most appropriate for a function 'calculateArea' that returns an integer and takes a float and a double?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option A indicates that 'calculateArea' returns an int and takes a float and a double as parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to define a default return type for a function if none is explicitly specified?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C, if no return type is specified for a function, it defaults to 'int'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a valid function definition in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option B correctly defines a function with a return type and a body.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does a function prototype provide to the compiler?

Options.OptionText: The return type and name of the function only
The function's name, return type, and parameter types

m_Item1.Explanation: A function prototype specifies the function’s name, return type, and parameters, informing the compiler of how to call the function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about function declarations and definitions is correct?

Options.OptionText: Definitions do not require the return type.
Declarations must precede their calls in a program.

m_Item1.Explanation: Declarations must be present before any call to the function to provide necessary information to the compiler.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a function is defined with a return type but lacks a return statement with a value?

Options.OptionText: The function will return garbage value.
The compiler will ignore the return type.

m_Item1.Explanation: If a function specifies a non-void return type but lacks a return statement, the function returns an indeterminate value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly defines a function in C with no parameters and returning nothing?
m_Item1.Explanation: The option C correctly defines a function without parameters and with a 'void' return type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which component is not essential in a function definition?
m_Item1.Explanation: Documentation is helpful but not essential for defining a function in C.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you define a function that takes a pointer to an integer as a parameter?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C, 'int* p' correctly declares a pointer to an integer as a parameter of a function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of using 'static' keyword in a function definition?

Options.OptionText: To restrict the visibility of the function to the file scope
To allow the function to return multiple values
To ensure the function runs only once

m_Item1.Explanation: Using 'static' restricts the function's scope to the file it is declared in, preventing external linkage.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following function declarations is correct for a function that adds two integers and returns the result?
m_Item1.Explanation: The declaration must specify the return type, function name, and parameter types, as shown in option D.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When declaring a function, which part tells the compiler that the function will not return any value?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'void' keyword indicates that the function does not return a value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true for C functions?

Options.OptionText: The main function cannot call other functions.
Functions can be used to avoid repetition of code.

m_Item1.Explanation: Functions help avoid code repetition by encapsulating reusable code blocks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Identify the correct statement about the scope of a function in C.

Options.OptionText: Functions have file scope by default.

m_Item1.Explanation: By default, functions defined without the 'static' keyword have file scope in C.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of the function's return type in its definition?

Options.OptionText: Indicates the type of variables it operates on
Specifies the data type of the result returned by the function

m_Item1.Explanation: The return type indicates the type of value the function will return to the caller.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following keywords cannot be used for a function definition in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'private' keyword is not a valid keyword in C for defining functions; it's used in languages like C++ for classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In what way does forward declaration of functions help in C programming?

Options.OptionText: It allows functions to be defined in any order.

m_Item1.Explanation: Forward declarations allow functions to be called before they are defined, facilitating more flexible coding.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is NOT a return type that a function can use?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'entire' is not a valid return type in C; it should be 'int'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a function signature include the const keyword, and if so, what does it signify?

Options.OptionText: Yes, it makes the return value constant.
No, const can only be used with variables.
Yes, it indicates the function will not modify the object passed.

m_Item1.Explanation: Using 'const' in a function signature signifies that the function will not alter the passed reference or pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes a C function definition?

Options.OptionText: A declaration that includes parameter names and types.
A statement that ends with a semicolon.
A block of code that specifies what the function does.
A statement that initializes function variables.

m_Item1.Explanation: A function definition is a block of code that contains the actual implementation of what the function does.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following C code?
#include <stdio.h>
void display();
int main() {
    display();
    return 0;
}
void display() {
    printf("Hello World\n");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The display function is defined correctly and prints 'Hello World' when called from main.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the code, what is the output?
#include <stdio.h>
int increment(int a) {
    return a + 1;
}
int main() {
    printf("%d", increment(5));
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The increment function adds 1 to the argument 5 and returns 6, which is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you use a function without its prototype being declared in the code?

Options.OptionText: Function works normally if defined later

m_Item1.Explanation: Using a function before declaring it or defining its prototype causes a compilation error.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code?
#include <stdio.h>
void func(int a);
int main() {
    func(5);
    return 0;
}
void func(int a) {
    printf("%d", a);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Function func is properly defined and prints the passed parameter, 5.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C, what is the correct way to declare a function that returns a float and takes an integer as an argument?
m_Item1.Explanation: The syntax float functionName(int); declares a function that returns a float and takes an integer as an argument.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this function call if x is 10?
#include <stdio.h>
void func(int x) {
    printf("%d", x);
}
int main() {
    func(10);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The function func is properly called with 10 as argument, and prints 10.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you call a function named 'compute' that returns an integer value and takes two float parameters?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option D correctly calls the function 'compute' with the required float parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following program?
#include <stdio.h>
int sum(int a, int b);
int main() {
    int result = sum(3, 7);
    printf("%d", result);
    return 0;
}
int sum(int a, int b) {
    return a + b;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The sum function returns the sum of its two parameters, 3 and 7, resulting in 10.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the consequence of not returning a value from a non-void function?
m_Item1.Explanation: Not returning a value from a non-void function leads to undefined behavior as the function is expected to return a valid value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a function modifies one of its parameters, how does it affect the original argument passed to it?

Options.OptionText: No effect on the original argument

m_Item1.Explanation: In C, function arguments are passed by value, meaning modifications do not affect the original arguments.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does 'void' in function declaration mean?

Options.OptionText: Function takes a variable number of arguments
Function takes a single integer argument

m_Item1.Explanation: In C, 'void' in a function declaration signifies that the function does not return any value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of function calls, what is the stack primarily used for?

Options.OptionText: Storing local variables and function call information
Storing memory addresses of dynamic allocations

m_Item1.Explanation: The stack stores local variables and function call information during program execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does 'int (*funcPtr)(int)' represent?

Options.OptionText: A pointer to a function that returns an integer
A pointer to an int variable
A function returning a pointer to int

m_Item1.Explanation: This syntax represents a pointer to a function that takes an integer argument and returns an integer.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of this code?
#include <stdio.h>
void test();
int main() {
    test();
    test();
    return 0;
}
void test() {
    static int x = 0;
    printf("%d", x);
    x++;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Static variables retain their value between function calls, thus the output is '01'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of this code?
#include <stdio.h>
void greet(char* msg) {
    printf("%s\n", msg);
}
int main() {
    greet("Hello");
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The function greet is called with "Hello" and successfully prints it.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given the function prototype 'int compute(float x, int y);', which of these is NOT a correct function call?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option C is incorrect because the arguments are not automatically converted as required (float first then int).

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the following C program print?
#include <stdio.h>
int foo() {
    return 5;
}
int main() {
    printf("%d", foo()+5);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Function foo returns 5, which is added to 5 in main, resulting in the output 10.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of 'return' in functions in C programming?

Options.OptionText: To stop the execution of the program
To print the value on the console
To terminate the function and return a value to the caller

m_Item1.Explanation: 'return' is used to terminate a function and return control to the caller along with a value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the result of calling a void function in an assignment expression?

Options.OptionText: The assignment is valid with a default value
Nothing, void return type cannot be assigned

m_Item1.Explanation: Attempting to assign the result of a void function will result in a compilation error because void functions do not return a value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you define a function that takes no arguments and returns a pointer to int?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option C correctly declares a function that takes no arguments and returns a pointer to int.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code snippet?
#include <stdio.h>
void multiply(int a, int b) {
    printf("%d", a * b);
}
int main() {
    multiply(4, 5);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The multiply function is called with parameters 4 and 5, so it prints 4 * 5 = 20.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the correct way to declare a function that takes two float parameters and returns an int?
m_Item1.Explanation: A function that takes two float arguments and returns an int should be declared as 'int function(float a, float b);'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the result of the following code?
#include <stdio.h>
void add(int a, int b) {
    a += 2;
    b += 2;
    printf("%d %d", a, b);
}
int main() {
    int x = 5, y = 3;
    add(x, y);
    printf("%d %d", x, y);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Within add(), separate copies of a and b are modified, but these changes do not affect the original variables x and y.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code snippet:
#include <stdio.h>
int sum(int, int);
int main() {
    printf("%d", sum(2, 3));
    return 0;
}
int sum(int a, int b) {
    return a + b;
}
What does the code output?
m_Item1.Explanation: The sum function returns the result of adding its two parameters, 2 + 3, which is 5.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of the parameters in a function?

Options.OptionText: To define local variables within a function
To pass information or arguments to a function
To indicate the function's data type

m_Item1.Explanation: Parameters are used to pass information or arguments into functions so they can operate on them.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function declaration is correct if the function is supposed to modify the values of two integer variables by adding them?
m_Item1.Explanation: Using pointers (int *a, int *b), we can modify the values of the variables passed as parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement regarding passing arguments by value is true?

Options.OptionText: Passing by value uses pointer variables.
Passing by value directly alters the original data.

m_Item1.Explanation: When arguments are passed by value, changes inside the function do not affect the original data.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the output be for the following code?
#include <stdio.h>
void foo(int x, int y, int z) {
    printf("%d", z);
}
int main() {
    foo(2, 3, 4);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The parameter z is printed, which was passed the value 4 in the function call.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a correct pointer function signature, for a function that takes a pointer to an integer and returns a pointer to an integer?
m_Item1.Explanation: The function signature indicates it takes an int pointer and returns an int pointer: 'int* function(int*);'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What type of function is needed when arguments must be modified by the function?
m_Item1.Explanation: A function must accept pointers if it needs to modify arguments since it allows access to the actual memory address.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What does the following code demonstrate?
#include <stdio.h>
void swap(int *a, int *b) {
    int temp = *a;
    *a = *b;
    *b = temp;
}
int main() {
    int x = 10, y = 20;
    swap(&x, &y);
    printf("%d %d", x, y);
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Swapping values using pass by reference
Swapping values using pass by value

m_Item1.Explanation: The function swap() uses pointers to swap the values of x and y, demonstrating pass by reference.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which situation would you choose to pass parameters by value instead of by reference in a function?

Options.OptionText: When the parameter is a large data structure
When the parameter is small and shouldn't be changed
When the function is supposed to modify original data
When you want to avoid creating a copy of the parameter

m_Item1.Explanation: Passing by value is chosen when the parameter is small and its values should not be modified outside the function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
How can you modify the following function to prevent altering the original arguments passed?
void increment(int *a, int *b) {
    (*a)++;
    (*b)++;
}

Options.OptionText: Change the function to void increment(int a, int b)
Use '&' instead of '*' to access the values.

m_Item1.Explanation: By changing the function parameters to pass by value, the original arguments will not be modified.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which parameter passing method is being used in the following snippet?
#include <stdio.h>
void process(float a) {
    a = a * a;
}
int main() {
    float x = 5.0;
    process(x);
    printf("%.2f", x);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Pass by value is being used, meaning the modifications in process() do not affect the original x.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a function declaration, what does the keyword 'void' specify when used in place of parameter types?

Options.OptionText: The function's parameters can be of any type

m_Item1.Explanation: When 'void' is used in the parameter list, it means the function does not accept any parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When is it more beneficial to use 'const' for function parameters?

Options.OptionText: When the parameter is large and shouldn't be modified
When the parameter must be modified
When the parameter is an int
Always with pointers to ensure modification

m_Item1.Explanation: 'const' is used with parameters, particularly large data structures, to prevent them from being modified.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output for following code?
#include <stdio.h>
int decrement(int a, int b) {
    return a - b;
}
int main() {
    int a = 5, b = 10;
    printf("%d", decrement(b, a));
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The function subtracts the second argument from the first, thus decrement(10, 5) returns 5.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is required to modify an array passed to a function so that changes persist after the function ends?

Options.OptionText: Copying the array within the function

m_Item1.Explanation: Using pointers allows array modifications to persist beyond a function call.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the function: void foo(int a, int b) {
    int temp = a;
    a = b;
    b = temp;
}
How would you correctly call this function to swap values?

Options.OptionText: Swap is not possible with current signature

m_Item1.Explanation: This signature does not use reference or pointers and cannot swap values. It needs pointer parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code?
#include <stdio.h>
void scale(int *a, int factor) {
    *a *= factor;
}
int main() {
    int y = 3;
    scale(&y, 2);
    printf("%d", y);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The scale function multiplies the value pointed to by a by factor: 3 * 2 = 6.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the output be when the following code is executed?
#include <stdio.h>
void setZero(int a, int b) {
    a = 0;
    b = 0;
}
int main() {
    int x = 1, y = 2;
    setZero(x, y);
    printf("%d %d", x, y);
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: 'setZero' modifies only local copies of x and y, so x and y in main remain unchanged.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of returning a value from a function?

Options.OptionText: To print the result to the console
To provide the result back to the calling code
To declare success of function execution

m_Item1.Explanation: Returning a value from a function provides the result back to the calling code for further use.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code?
int add(int a, int b) {
    return a + b;
}
int main() {
    int result = add(5, 7);
    printf("%d", result);
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: It will cause a compile-time error
It will cause a runtime error

m_Item1.Explanation: The function add takes two integers, adds them, and returns the result, which is then printed as 12.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if you forget to include a return statement in a non-void function?

Options.OptionText: The function will return 0 by default
The function will return -1 by default

m_Item1.Explanation: Forgetting a return statement in a non-void function results in undefined behavior according to C standard.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following function declarations is correct?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option B is the correct syntax for declaring a function with an integer return type and two integer parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the code 'int multiply(int a, int b) { return a * b; }', what is the return type of the multiply function?
m_Item1.Explanation: The multiply function has a return type of int, as specified in the function declaration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true about a function with return type void?

Options.OptionText: It must include a return statement
It can return a value but does not have to
The return type of void allows for any data type

m_Item1.Explanation: A function with a return type of void cannot return any value.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the result of following code?
int increment(int a) {
    return a + 1;
}
int main() {
    printf("%d", increment(10));
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The increment function adds 1 to its argument and returns the result, so increment(10) returns 11.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the function:
int sum(int a, int b) {
    int result = a + b;
}
Will the function compile and return correctly if called in the main function?

Options.OptionText: Yes, it will compile and return correctly
No, it lacks a return statement
No, the return type should be void

m_Item1.Explanation: The function sum lacks a return statement, which is necessary since its return type is int.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the code snippet:
char getFirstChar(char *str) {
    return str[0];
}
What is the return type of the getFirstChar function?
m_Item1.Explanation: The getFirstChar function has a return type of char, as specified in its declaration.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the following code output?
double getHalf(int num) {
    return num / 2;
}
int main() {
    printf("%.1f", getHalf(9));
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The getHalf function performs integer division, returning 4. The return type doesn't change the division result.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is a valid function signature for a function returning a pointer to an int?
m_Item1.Explanation: The correct syntax for a function returning a pointer to an int is 'int *func();'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Examine the code:
double squareRoot(int value) {
    return sqrt(value);
}
What library must be included for a successful compile?
m_Item1.Explanation: Mathematical functions like sqrt are available in the <math.h> library, which must be included.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Choose the appropriate return type for a function designed to return a character from a string based on an index.
m_Item1.Explanation: The function should return a single character, thus the return type should be char.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is correct for a function declared as: int func(void)?
m_Item1.Explanation: The declaration 'int func(void)' specifies a function that takes no arguments and returns an int.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the function:
int findMax(int a, int b) {
    return (a > b) ? a : b;
}
What is the return type of findMax?
m_Item1.Explanation: The function findMax returns an int, as indicated by its return type in the declaration.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What value will be returned by foo in the code snippet?
int foo() {
    return 5.5;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The function foo returns an integer, so the float 5.5 is implicitly converted to 5.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following code snippets will cause a compile-time error due to return type issues?
m_Item1.Explanation: A function with a non-void return type must return a value; thus, return; in a float function causes an error.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the code print?
float toDouble(int num) {
    return (double)num;
}
int main() {
    printf("%.2f", toDouble(3));
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The function casts an int to a double and returns it. Printing as %.2f gives 3.00.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a void function attempts to return a value?
m_Item1.Explanation: Returning a value from a void function causes a compilation error, since such functions cannot return values.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Examine the function declaration:
int* findMax(int [], int);
Which is an accurate description of what this function returns?
m_Item1.Explanation: The function findMax returns a pointer to an integer, as indicated by the return type 'int*'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What does the following function return when passed 0 as an argument?
float selector(int choice) {
    if(choice) return 10.5;
    return;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Returning without a value in a non-void function causes a compile-time error in C.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is not a valid return type for a C function?
m_Item1.Explanation: C does not support a return type of string natively. Strings are typically represented as char arrays or char pointers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the use of function?

Options.OptionText: Helps to avoid repeating a set of statements many times.
Enhances the logical clarity of the program.

m_Item1.Explanation: All of the mentioned are uses of function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is used to send a value back from a function?
m_Item1.Explanation: Return statement in the function takes te control back to the calling function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void fun(int a)
{
    a = a + 10;
}
int main() {
    int x = 5;
    fun(x);
    printf("%d", x);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Pass by value

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following function declarations is correct?

Options.OptionText: declare int fun(int a, int b);

m_Item1.Explanation: B option gives correct syntax.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about static variables inside functions?

Options.OptionText: They are destroyed when function exits
They keep their value between function calls

m_Item1.Explanation: Static is a storage class and these variables retain the value between the fuction calls. They are initialized only once.
